preamble.summary: A psalm of David expressing defiant trust in the LORD when counseled to flee. The psalmist refuses escape, grounding his confidence in the LORD's heavenly throne and his examination of the righteous and the wicked. The psalm moves from the temptation to run to the certainty of divine justice, ending with the vision of the upright beholding God's face.
preamble.remarkable: The psalm opens with a quotation from fearful advisors — 'Flee to your mountain like a bird' — and then spends the rest of its brief length demolishing the premise behind their advice. The logic is architectural: the LORD is in his holy temple, the LORD's throne is in heaven, therefore the foundations have not been destroyed no matter what the wicked do. The final line — 'the upright will behold his face' — uses the language of temple access and royal audience. To see the face of the king was the highest privilege in the ancient Near East. The psalm promises that the righteous will receive what was denied even to Moses (Exodus 33:20): the direct vision of God.
preamble.friction: Verse 3 — 'When the foundations are destroyed, what can the righteous do?' — is ambiguous. It may still be part of the advisors' fearful counsel, or it may be the psalmist's own rhetorical question. The Hebrew ki ('when, if, because') leaves the relationship between verses 2-3 deliberately open. The 'foundations' (shatot) likely refer to the foundations of social and moral order, not literal architecture. The word appears only here and in Psalm 82:5, where it describes the cosmic foundations shaking when justice fails.
preamble.connections: The imagery of the LORD testing from his temple connects to Habakkuk 2:20 ('The LORD is in his holy temple; let all the earth keep silence'). The fire and brimstone judgment on the wicked (v. 6) echoes the destruction of Sodom in Genesis 19:24. The concept of beholding God's face reappears in Psalm 17:15, which closes with almost identical language. The bird-flight metaphor recurs in Psalm 55:6-8, where David wishes for wings to fly away — a wish this psalm refuses.

verses.text_hebrew: לַמְנַצֵּ֗חַ לְדָ֫וִ֥ד בַּֽיהוָ֨ה ׀ חָסִ֗יתִי אֵ֭יךְ תֹּאמְר֣וּ לְנַפְשִׁ֑י נ֛֝וּדִי הַרְכֶ֥ם צִפּֽוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: In the LORD put I my trust: how say ye to my soul, Flee as a bird to your mountain?
verses.rendering:
For the choirmaster. Of David.
In the LORD I have taken refuge.
How can you say to my soul,
'Flee to your mountain like a bird'?
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with the superscription assigning it to David and to the choirmaster (lamnatseach), indicating liturgical use. The first declaration — ba-YHVH chasiti ('in the LORD I have taken refuge') — establishes the entire psalm's foundation before the challenge is even quoted. The verb chasah ('to take refuge, to seek shelter') is a covenant term: it describes the act of placing oneself under God's protection, as a bird shelters under a wing (Psalm 36:7). The irony is sharp: the advisors tell the psalmist to fly away like a bird, but the psalmist has already found shelter — not on a mountain, but in the LORD himself. The plural 'you say' (tomru) indicates multiple voices urging flight.
translator_notes: lamnatseach is traditionally rendered 'To the chief musician' (KJV) but more likely means 'for the choirmaster' or 'for the musical director,' indicating the psalm was assigned to the temple music leader for liturgical performance.

The verb chasah ('to take refuge') is distinct from batach ('to trust'). It implies active movement toward shelter — not passive confidence but deliberate flight to God rather than to the mountains. The wordplay is intentional: the advisors say 'flee' (nudi), but the psalmist has already fled — to the LORD.

key_terms.semantic_range: to seek refuge, take shelter, flee for protection, trust by hiding in
key_terms.note: chasah appears frequently in the Psalms (over 25 times) as the primary verb for seeking divine protection. It carries the physical image of sheltering under something — a rock, a wing, a fortress. The perfect tense here (chasiti) indicates a completed action: the psalmist has already taken refuge. The decision is made before the advisors even speak.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י הִנֵּ֪ה הָרְשָׁעִ֡ים יִדְרְכ֬וּן קֶ֗שֶׁת כּוֹנְנ֣וּ חִצָּ֣ם עַל־יֶ֑תֶר לִיר֥וֹת בְּמוֹ־אֹ֝֗פֶל לְיִשְׁרֵי־לֵֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: For, lo, the wicked bend their bow, they make ready their arrow upon the string, that they may privily shoot at the upright in heart.
verses.rendering:
For look — the wicked bend the bow,
they fit their arrow to the string
to shoot from the shadows
at the upright in heart.
translator_notes: The phrase be-mo ofel ('in/from darkness') indicates covert attack — the wicked operate in concealment, ambushing those who live openly. The 'upright in heart' (yishrei lev) describes people whose inner disposition is straight and honest, making them vulnerable to those who operate by deception.
This verse may still be part of the advisors' speech — they describe the danger to justify their counsel to flee. The ki hinneh ('for look!') adds urgency to their warning.

verses.text_kjv: If the foundations be destroyed, what can the righteous do?
verses.rendering:
When the foundations are torn down,
what can the righteous do?
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the climax of the fearful counsel — and perhaps the most haunting question in the psalm. The shatot ('foundations') refer not to building foundations but to the bedrock of social order, justice, and moral structure. The verb yeharesun ('are being destroyed, torn down') is a Niphal imperfect suggesting ongoing demolition: the foundations are in the process of collapse. The question mah pa'al tsaddiq ('what has the righteous accomplished?' or 'what can the righteous do?') could be read as despair or as a genuine theological challenge. If the structures of justice are gone, what recourse do the righteous have? The psalmist's answer begins in the next verse: the LORD is still on his throne. The earthly foundations may shake, but the heavenly ones hold.
translator_notes: The word shatot ('foundations') occurs only here and in Psalm 82:5 in the entire Hebrew Bible. In Psalm 82 the foundations of the earth totter because divine judges have failed to do justice. The same concept applies here: when the structures that uphold justice collapse, the righteous seem powerless.

Some interpreters read this verse as still quoting the advisors (making vv. 1c-3 a single quotation). Others see it as the psalmist's own question, answered by vv. 4-7. The ambiguity may be intentional.

key_terms.semantic_range: foundations, bases, pillars of social or cosmic order
key_terms.note: A rare word appearing only twice in the Hebrew Bible. It derives from shatah ('to set, establish') and refers to the fundamental structures that hold society or the cosmos in place. When shatot collapse, it is not a building that falls but the moral order itself.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֤ה ׀ בְּֽהֵ֘יכַ֤ל קׇדְשׁ֗וֹ יְהוָה֮ בַּשָּׁמַ֢יִם כִּ֫סְא֥וֹ עֵינָ֥יו יֶחֱז֑וּ עַפְעַפָּ֥יו יִ֝בְחֲנ֗וּ בְּנֵ֣י אָדָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD is in his holy temple, the LORD'S throne is in heaven: his eyes behold, his eyelids try, the children of men.
verses.rendering:
The LORD is in his holy temple;
the LORD — his throne is in heaven.
His eyes observe,
his eyelids examine the children of humanity.
translator_notes: The word af'appayim ('eyelids') is unusual in describing divine sight. It may suggest the narrowing of eyes in intense scrutiny, or it may be a poetic parallel for 'eyes' without additional meaning. The image is anthropomorphic but functions to convey the thoroughness of divine observation.

heikhal can mean both 'temple' and 'palace' — the ambiguity is productive, since the LORD's temple is also his throne room. The heavenly temple and the earthly sanctuary are linked in Israelite theology.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֤ה צַדִּ֣יק יִבְחָ֑ן וְ֝רָשָׁ֗ע וְאֹהֵ֥ב חָמָ֗ס שָֽׂנְאָ֥ה נַפְשֽׁוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD trieth the righteous: but the wicked and him that loveth violence his soul hateth.
verses.rendering:
The LORD examines the righteous,
but the wicked and the lover of violence
his soul hates.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb yivchan ('examines, tests') from the previous verse now takes on moral specificity. The LORD tests the righteous — the verb implies refining, as a metalworker tests ore to reveal its purity. But the wicked and the lover of violence (ohev chamas) receive something different: hatred from God's own soul (nafsho). The text does not soften this. The LORD does not merely disapprove of violence; his very being (nefesh) hates those who love it. The parallelism reveals a sharp distinction: the righteous are tested (refined, purified through difficulty), while the wicked are condemned. The testing of the righteous is not punishment but process — the hatred of the wicked is verdict.
translator_notes: The phrase sane'ah nafsho ('his soul hates') is among the strongest expressions of divine disapproval in the Psalter. nefesh here means the essential self, the core of God's being. This is not casual dislike but visceral rejection.

chamas ('violence') is the same word used in Genesis 6:11 to describe the corruption that provoked the flood. It denotes not merely physical force but the violation of social bonds, the breakdown of trust between human beings.

key_terms.semantic_range: violence, wrongdoing, injustice, cruelty, violation of social order
key_terms.note: chamas encompasses more than physical assault. It includes fraud, oppression, and any act that violates the bonds of community. In Genesis 6:11, it is the sin that fills the earth before the flood. Here it characterizes the wicked whom God's soul hates.

verses.text_hebrew: יַמְטֵ֥ר עַל־רְשָׁעִ֗ים פַּ֫חִ֥ים אֵ֣שׁ וְ֭גׇפְרִית וְר֥וּחַ זִלְעָפ֗וֹת מְנָ֣ת כּוֹסָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Upon the wicked he shall rain snares, fire and brimstone, and an horrible tempest: this shall be the portion of their cup.
verses.expanded_rendering: The judgment imagery is volcanic and deliberate. The verb yamter ('he rains') echoes Genesis 19:24, where the LORD rained (himtir) fire and sulfur on Sodom and Gomorrah. The psalm places the wicked under the same judgment as the cities of the plain. The word pachim ('snares' or 'burning coals' — the meaning is debated) adds either entrapment or combustion to the picture. The final phrase — menat kosam ('the portion of their cup') — introduces the metaphor of the cup of judgment, a recurring biblical image where God's wrath is poured like a drink that the wicked must consume. The cup is their lot, their inheritance, their assigned portion. Where the righteous inherit the land, the wicked inherit fire.
translator_notes: pachim is difficult. It may mean 'snares, traps' (from pach) or 'burning coals' (from pecham). The context of fire and sulfur favors 'burning coals,' but the ambiguity may be intentional — the wicked are both trapped and burned.
The 'cup' (kos) as a metaphor for divine judgment appears in Isaiah 51:17, Jeremiah 25:15, and Psalm 75:8. It conveys inevitability: you must drink what is poured for you.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־צַדִּ֣יק יְ֭הוָה צְדָק֣וֹת אָהֵ֑ב יָ֝שָׁ֗ר יֶחֱז֥וּ פָנֵֽימוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: For the righteous LORD loveth righteousness; his countenance doth behold the upright.
verses.rendering:
For the LORD is righteous — he loves righteous deeds.
The upright will behold his face.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final verse provides the theological foundation for everything that preceded it. The LORD is tsaddiq ('righteous'), and therefore he loves tsedaqot ('righteous acts, deeds of justice'). The correspondence is complete: a righteous God loves righteousness. But the stunning payoff comes in the second line: yashar yechezu fanemo ('the upright will behold his face'). The subject shifts — it is no longer God gazing at humanity (as in v. 4) but the upright gazing at God. The verb chazah ('to see, behold, envision') suggests penetrating vision, prophetic sight. And panemo ('his face') is the ultimate reward: to see the face of God is to be in his unmediated presence. In the ancient Near East, seeing the king's face meant being admitted to the inner court. The psalm ends with the righteous admitted to the presence of the divine King — not fleeing to mountains but standing before the throne.
translator_notes: The final line is grammatically ambiguous: fanemo could mean 'his face' (God's face that the upright behold) or 'their face' (God beholds the face of the upright). Most Hebrew scholars read it as 'his face' — the upright will see God. This reading provides the climactic reversal: the psalm began with the temptation to flee God's world, and it ends with the promise of seeing God's face.

The shift from God examining humanity (v. 4) to humanity beholding God (v. 7) creates a reciprocal gaze — God sees us, and we will see God. This mutual vision is the psalm's definition of the covenant relationship.

key_terms.semantic_range: righteousness, righteous acts, justice, vindication, saving acts
key_terms.note: The plural tsedaqot refers to concrete acts of righteousness rather than abstract righteousness (tsedaqah). God loves not merely the concept of justice but its practice — specific deeds that conform to the covenant order.

key_terms.semantic_range: face, presence, countenance, the front or visible aspect of a person
key_terms.note: panim ('face') in relation to God signifies his direct, unmediated presence. To see God's face is the highest blessing (Numbers 6:25-26) and the greatest danger (Exodus 33:20). This psalm promises that the upright will receive what Moses was denied.
